The tension in the string, when the stone is at the lowest point is (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)

solution:
Given that 

Given the mass m=900 g=9001000 kg=910 kg,

The radius of the circular path r=1 m

And, the angular velocity

ω=2πN60=2π(10)60=π3 rad s-1

With reference to the above diagram, the formula to calculate the tension at the lowermost point can be written as

T-mg=mrω2⇒T=mg+mrω2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

T=910×9.8+910×1π32=8.82+910×π29=8.82+0.98=9.8 N

subject: Physics
q: A cubic block of mass $m$ is sliding down on an inclined plane at $60^{\circ}$ with an acceleration of $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}$, the value of coefficient of kinetic friction is
question: A cubic block of mass $m$ is sliding down on an inclined plane at $60^{\circ}$ with an acceleration of $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}$, the value of coefficient of kinetic friction is
solution:
$\mathrm{mg} \sin 60^{\circ}-\mu \mathrm{mg} \cos 60^{\circ}=\mathrm{ma}$
$g \sin 60-\mu g \cos 60=\frac{g}{2}$
$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{\mu}{2}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\mu=\sqrt{3}-1$

subject: Physics
q: A car of mass $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving at a speed of 30 $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. Brakes are applied to bring the car to rest. If the net retarding force is $5000 \mathrm{~N}$, the car comes to stop after travelling $d \mathrm{~m}$ in $t \mathrm{~s}$. Then
question: A car of mass $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving at a speed of 30 $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. Brakes are applied to bring the car to rest. If the net retarding force is $5000 \mathrm{~N}$, the car comes to stop after travelling $d \mathrm{~m}$ in $t \mathrm{~s}$. Then
solution:
Given: mass of car $m=1000 \mathrm{~kg}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& u=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& v=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \text { retarding force } f=5000 \mathrm{~N} \\
& \therefore \text { retardation, }-a=\frac{5000}{1000}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
By equation, $v^2-u^2=2$ as
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0-(30)^2=-2 \times 5 \times d \\
& \therefore d=\frac{900}{10}=90 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$
and $a=\frac{v-u}{t} \therefore t=\frac{v-u}{a}=\frac{0-30}{-5}=6 \mathrm{~s}$

subject: Physics
q:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
If two springs $\mathrm{S_1}$ and $\mathrm{S_2}$ of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively, are stretched by the same force, it is found that more work is done on spring $\mathrm{S_1}$ than on spring $\mathrm{S_2}$.
Statement $1$ : If stretched by the same amount, work done on $\mathrm{S}_1$, will be more than that on $\mathrm{S}_2$
Statement $2: k_1 < k_2$
question:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$. Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
If two springs $\mathrm{S_1}$ and $\mathrm{S_2}$ of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively, are stretched by the same force, it is found that more work is done on spring $\mathrm{S_1}$ than on spring $\mathrm{S_2}$.
Statement $1$ : If stretched by the same amount, work done on $\mathrm{S}_1$, will be more than that on $\mathrm{S}_2$
Statement $2: k_1 < k_2$
options: Statement $1$ is false, Statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is false
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is the correct explanation for statement $1$
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is true, Statement $2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $1$.
solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{S}_1=\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{S}_2$
$\mathrm{W}_1=\mathrm{FS}_1, \mathrm{~W}_2=\mathrm{FS}_2$
$\mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{S}_1{ }^2>\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{S}_2^2$
$\mathrm{S}_1>\mathrm{S}_2$
$\mathrm{K}_1 < \mathrm{K}_2$
$\mathrm{W} \propto \mathrm{K}$
$\mathrm{W}_1 < \mathrm{W}_2$

subject: Physics
q: A large drum having radius $R$ is spinning around its axis with angular velocity $\omega$, as shown in figure. The minimum value of $\omega$ so that a body of mass $M$ remains stuck to the inner wall of the drum, taking the coefficient of friction between the drum surface and mass $M$ as $\mu$, is :
question: A large drum having radius $R$ is spinning around its axis with angular velocity $\omega$, as shown in figure. The minimum value of $\omega$ so that a body of mass $M$ remains stuck to the inner wall of the drum, taking the coefficient of friction between the drum surface and mass $M$ as $\mu$, is :
solution:
Consider the mass $M$ in the frame of the rotating drum. The forces acting on the mass are:
1. Weight $Mg$ acting vertically downwards.
2. Normal reaction $N$ from the wall acting towards the center, which provides the centripetal force: $N = M\omega^2 R$.
3. Frictional force $f$ acting vertically upwards to balance the weight.
For the mass to remain stuck to the wall, the frictional force must balance the weight: $f = Mg$.
The maximum available static friction is $f_{max} = \mu N = \mu M\omega^2 R$.
To prevent slipping, we must have $f_{max} \ge Mg$.
$\mu M\omega^2 R \ge Mg$
$\omega^2 \ge \frac{g}{\mu R}$
$\omega \ge \sqrt{\frac{g}{\mu R}}$
The minimum value of $\omega$ is $\sqrt{\frac{g}{\mu R}}$.

solution:
Fcosθ=μN

⇒Fsinθ+N=mg

⇒F=μmgcosθ+μsinθ

Fmin=μmg1+μ2=13×1023=5

To negotiate a curve radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, the height by which the outer rail should be raised with respect to the inner rail is (Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>):
solution:
For the train, moving along the curved rails, it can be written, with reference to the figure below, that

Also, from the above figure, it follows that

subject: Physics
q:
A block of mass 5 kg is moving on an inclined plane which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Friction coefficient between the block and inclined plane surface is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. The force to be applied on the block so that the block will move down without acceleration is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
$\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$.
question:
A block of mass 5 kg is moving on an inclined plane which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Friction coefficient between the block and inclined plane surface is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. The force to be applied on the block so that the block will move down without acceleration is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
$\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}\right)$.
solution:
For motion without acceleration, net force = 0.
Component of weight along incline: $mg\sin(30°) = 5 \times 10 \times 0.5 = 25$ N (down).
Normal force: $N = mg\cos(30°) = 50 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 25\sqrt{3}$ N.
Friction force opposing downward motion:
$f = \mu N = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 25\sqrt{3} = 37.5$ N (up).
For equilibrium along the incline, applying force F down the incline:
$mg\sin\theta + F = f$, so $25 + F = 37.5$, giving $F = 12.5$ N.

subject: Physics
q: A small block of mass 100 g is tied to a spring of spring constant 7.5 N m-1 and length 20 cm. The other end of spring is fixed at a particular point A. If the block moves in a circular path on a smooth horizontal surface with constant angular velocity 5 rad s-1about point A, then tension in the spring is
question: A small block of mass 100 g is tied to a spring of spring constant 7.5 N m-1 and length 20 cm. The other end of spring is fixed at a particular point A. If the block moves in a circular path on a smooth horizontal surface with constant angular velocity 5 rad s-1about point A, then tension in the spring is
solution:
Centripetal force will be provided by the spring force. Let the elongation in the spring be x, then we can write

⇒kx=mω2(r+x)

⇒7.5 x=2.5(0.2+x)

⇒x=0.55=0.1

Therefore, required tension will beT=kx=0.75 N.

q: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is fired on a wooden block to pierce through it, but it stops after moving a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> into it. If the velocity of the bullet before hitting the wood is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and, it slows down with uniform deceleration, then the magnitude of effective retarding force on the bullet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is,
question: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is fired on a wooden block to pierce through it, but it stops after moving a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> into it. If the velocity of the bullet before hitting the wood is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and, it slows down with uniform deceleration, then the magnitude of effective retarding force on the bullet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is,
solution:
v2=u2+2as⇒0=(10)2+2(-a)12
⇒a=100 m s-2
⇒F=ma=(0.1)(100)=10 N

question: A block of mass m slides along a floor while a force of magnitude F is applied to it at an angle θ as shown in figure. The coefficient of kinetic friction is μK. Then, the block's acceleration a is given by : (g is acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
N=mg-fsinθ

Fcosθ-μkN=ma

Fcosθ-μkmg-Fsinθ=ma

a=Fmcosθ-μKg-Fmsinθ

subject: Physics
q: Two billiard balls of equal mass 30 g strike a rigid wall with same speed of 108 kmph (as shown) but at different angles. If the balls get reflected with the same speed, then the ratio of the magnitude of impulses imparted to ball a and ball b by the wall along X direction is:
question: Two billiard balls of equal mass 30 g strike a rigid wall with same speed of 108 kmph (as shown) but at different angles. If the balls get reflected with the same speed, then the ratio of the magnitude of impulses imparted to ball a and ball b by the wall along X direction is:
solution:
Impulse = change in momentum

Ball (a) |Δp→|=2mu=J1

Ball (b) |Δp→|=2mucos45°=J2

J1 J2=1cos45°=2

q:
A block of mass 200 g is kept stationary on a smooth inclined plane by applying a minimum horizontal force F=x N as shown in figure.

The value of x=_____.
question:
A block of mass 200 g is kept stationary on a smooth inclined plane by applying a minimum horizontal force F=x N as shown in figure.

The value of x=_____.
solution:
Balancing the forces in horizontal direction Nsinθ=F

And balancing the forces in vertical direction Ncosθ=mg

⇒tanθ=Fmg⇒F=mgtanθ

F=0.2×10×3=12 N. Therefore, x=12

q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The body stops after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> due to friction between body and the floor. The value of the coefficient of friction is: (Take acceleration due to gravity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moving with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The body stops after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> due to friction between body and the floor. The value of the coefficient of friction is: (Take acceleration due to gravity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Friction force wil act opposite to the motion of the body.

F=μN=μmg

⇒a=-μg-ve sign for direction relative to the motion of body

Now, applying equation of motion, we get

v=u+at

⇒0=20+-μ×10×5

⇒50μ=20

 ⇒μ=0.4

subject: Physics
q: A massless spring gets elongated by amount $x_1$ under a tension of 5 N . Its elongation is $x_2$ under the tension of 7 N . For the elongation of $\left(5 x_1-2 x_2\right)$, the tension in the spring will be,
question: A massless spring gets elongated by amount $x_1$ under a tension of 5 N . Its elongation is $x_2$ under the tension of 7 N . For the elongation of $\left(5 x_1-2 x_2\right)$, the tension in the spring will be,
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{kx}_1=5 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \mathrm{kx}_2=7 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \mathrm{k}\left(5 \mathrm{x}_1-2 \mathrm{x}_2\right)=5 \mathrm{kx}_1-2 \mathrm{kx}_2 \\ & =5 \times 5-2 \times 7=11 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A block of 3 kg is attached to a string whose other end is attached to the wall. An unknown force F is applied so that the string makes an angle of 30° with the wall. The tension T is : (Given g =10 m s–2)
question: A block of 3 kg is attached to a string whose other end is attached to the wall. An unknown force F is applied so that the string makes an angle of 30° with the wall. The tension T is : (Given g =10 m s–2)
solution:
From geometry, θ=30°.

For equilibrium,

Tcosθ=mg

⇒T=mgcos30°⇒T=3×1032

⇒T=20 N

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass $M$ is pulled along a horizontal frictionless surface by a rope of mass $\mathrm{m}$. If a force $\mathrm{P}$ is applied at the free end of the rope, the force exerted by the rope on the block is
question: A block of mass $M$ is pulled along a horizontal frictionless surface by a rope of mass $\mathrm{m}$. If a force $\mathrm{P}$ is applied at the free end of the rope, the force exerted by the rope on the block is
solution:
Since the displacement for both block and rope is same so, the acceleration must be same for both

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=(\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{a} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M}}$ $\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{PM}}{\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M}}$

question: The upper half of an inclined plane with inclination $\phi$ is perfectly smooth while the lower half is rough. A body starting from rest at the top will again come to rest at the bottom if the coefficient of friction for the lower half is given by
solution: $m g s \sin \phi=\mu m g \cos \phi \cdot \frac{s}{2}$

subject: Physics
q:
Statement I: A cyclist is moving on an unbanked road with a speed of 7 km h-1 and takes a sharp circular turn along a path of the radius of 2 m without reducing the speed. The static friction coefficient is 0.2. The cyclist will not slip and pass the curve g=9.8 m s-2

Statement II : If the road is banked at an angle of 45°, cyclist can cross the curve of 2 m radius with the speed of 18.5 km h-1 without slipping. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Statement I: A cyclist is moving on an unbanked road with a speed of 7 km h-1 and takes a sharp circular turn along a path of the radius of 2 m without reducing the speed. The static friction coefficient is 0.2. The cyclist will not slip and pass the curve g=9.8 m s-2

Statement II : If the road is banked at an angle of 45°, cyclist can cross the curve of 2 m radius with the speed of 18.5 km h-1 without slipping. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is incorrect and statement II is correct

Statement I is correct and statement II is
incorrect
Both statement I and statement II are false
Both statement I and statement II are true
solution:
Statement Ivmax=μRg=0.2×2×9.8

vmax=1.97 m s-1

7 km h-1=1.944 m s-1

Speed is lower than vmax, hence it can take safe
turn.

Statement II 

vmax=Rgtanθ+μ1-μtanθ

=2×9.81+0.21-0.2=5.42 m s-1

18.5 km h-1=5.14 m s-1

Speed is lower than vmax, hence it can take safe turn.

q: When forces $F_1, F_2, F_3$ are acting on a particle of mass $m$ such that $F_2$ and $F_3$ are mutually perpendicular, then the particle remains stationary. If the force $F_1$ is now removed then the acceleration of the particle is
question: When forces $F_1, F_2, F_3$ are acting on a particle of mass $m$ such that $F_2$ and $F_3$ are mutually perpendicular, then the particle remains stationary. If the force $F_1$ is now removed then the acceleration of the particle is
solution: Resultant of F2 and F3 is of magnitude F1.

subject: Physics
q: A mass of $\mathrm{M} \mathrm{kg}$ is suspended by a weightless string. The horizontal force that is required to displace it until the string makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the initial vertical direction is
question: A mass of $\mathrm{M} \mathrm{kg}$ is suspended by a weightless string. The horizontal force that is required to displace it until the string makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the initial vertical direction is
solution:
$\mathrm{F} \ell \sin 45=M g(\ell-\ell \cos 45)$
$F=M g(\sqrt{2}-1)$

subject: Physics
q: A $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ brick begins to slide over a surface which is inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to horizontal axis. The co-efficient of static friction between their surfaces is:
question: A $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ brick begins to slide over a surface which is inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to horizontal axis. The co-efficient of static friction between their surfaces is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{mg} \sin 45=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{L}} \\ & \mathrm{mg} \cos 45=\mathrm{N} \\ & \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mu_{\mathrm{s}} N \\ & \mu_{\mathrm{s}}=\tan 45=1 \end{aligned}$ or $\tan \theta=\mu_s$ ( $\theta$ is angle of repose) $\tan 45=\mu_{\mathrm{s}}=1$

q: Two masses A and B, each of mass M are fixed together by a massless spring, A force acts on the mass B as shown in figure. If the mass A starts moving away from mass B with acceleration a, then the acceleration of mass B will be :
question: Two masses A and B, each of mass M are fixed together by a massless spring, A force acts on the mass B as shown in figure. If the mass A starts moving away from mass B with acceleration a, then the acceleration of mass B will be :
solution:
acm=m1a1+m2a2m1+m2

F2M=Ma+MaB2M

aB=F-MaM

subject: Physics
q: A system of two blocks of masses m=2 kg and M=8 kg is placed on a smooth table as shown in figure. The coefficient of static friction between two blocks is 0.5. The maximum horizontal force F that can be applied to the block of mass M so that the blocks move together will be g=9.8 m s-2
question: A system of two blocks of masses m=2 kg and M=8 kg is placed on a smooth table as shown in figure. The coefficient of static friction between two blocks is 0.5. The maximum horizontal force F that can be applied to the block of mass M so that the blocks move together will be g=9.8 m s-2
solution:
Maximum acceleration of the upper block will be,

amax=μg=0.5×9.8=4.9 m s-2

Therefore, maximum force that can be applied on the lower block is,

Fmax=2+8×4.9=49 N

subject: Physics
q: A body weighing $13 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended by two strings $5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $12 \mathrm{~m}$ long, their other ends being fastened to the extremities of a rod $13 \mathrm{~m}$ long. If the rod be so held that the body hangs immediately below the middle point. The tensions in the strings are
question: A body weighing $13 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended by two strings $5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $12 \mathrm{~m}$ long, their other ends being fastened to the extremities of a rod $13 \mathrm{~m}$ long. If the rod be so held that the body hangs immediately below the middle point. The tensions in the strings are
solution: $\mathrm{T}_2 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)=\mathrm{T}_1 \cos \theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_1 \cos \theta=\mathrm{T}_2 \sin \theta$ $\mathrm{T}_1 \sin \theta+\mathrm{T}_2 \cos \theta=13$. $\because \mathrm{OC}=\mathrm{CA}=\mathrm{CB}$ $\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{AOC}=\angle \mathrm{OAC}$ and $\angle \mathrm{COB}=\angle \mathrm{OBC}$ $\therefore \sin \theta=\sin \mathrm{A}=\frac{5}{13}$ and $\cos \theta=\frac{12}{13}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{5}{12} \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_1=\frac{5}{12} \mathrm{~T}_2$ $\mathrm{~T}_2\left(\frac{5}{12} \cdot \frac{5}{13}+\frac{12}{13}\right)=13$ $\mathrm{~T}_2\left(\frac{169}{12 \cdot 13}\right)=13$ $\mathrm{~T}_2=12 \mathrm{~kgs} . \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_1=5 \mathrm{~kgs}$.

q: An insect is at the bottom of a hemispherical ditch of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. It crawls up the ditch but starts slipping after it is at height h from the bottom. If the coefficient of friction between the ground and the insect is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>:</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
mgsinθ=μmgcosθ

tanθ=μ=0.75

⇒θ=37

cosθ=R-hR

cos37=1-h1⇒h=0.2

subject: Physics
q: A disc with a flat small bottom beaker placed on it at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> from its center is revolving about an axis passing through the center and perpendicular to its plane with an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. The coefficient of static friction between the bottom of the beaker and the surface of the disc is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi></math>. The beaker will revolve with the disc if :
question: A disc with a flat small bottom beaker placed on it at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> from its center is revolving about an axis passing through the center and perpendicular to its plane with an angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. The coefficient of static friction between the bottom of the beaker and the surface of the disc is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>μ</mi></math>. The beaker will revolve with the disc if :
solution:
As the disc is revolving in circular motion about an axis passing through centre and perpendicular to its plane, thus centrifugal force must act on it. The forces acting on disc is shown below.

 From FBD

For circular motion, frictional force must balance the required centripetal force.

So, f=mω2R

⇒μN≥mω2R

⇒μmg≥mω2R

⇒R≤μgω2

subject: Physics
q:
A block is sliding down on an inclined plane of slope $\theta$ and at an instant $t=0$ this block is given an upward momentum so that it starts moving up on the inclined surface with velocity $u$. The distance $(S)$ travelled by the block before its velocity become zero, is $\_\_\_\_$.
($g=$ gravitational acceleration)
question:
A block is sliding down on an inclined plane of slope $\theta$ and at an instant $t=0$ this block is given an upward momentum so that it starts moving up on the inclined surface with velocity $u$. The distance $(S)$ travelled by the block before its velocity become zero, is $\_\_\_\_$.
($g=$ gravitational acceleration)
solution:
$a = -g\sin\theta$
$V^2 = U^2 + 2as$
$0 = u^2 - 2g\sin\theta \cdot s$
$s = \dfrac{u^2}{2g\sin\theta}$

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving on a horizontal surface with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> enters a rough surface ranging from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The retarding force in this range of rough surface is related to distance by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>x</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The speed of the block as it just crosses the rough surface will be
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving on a horizontal surface with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> enters a rough surface ranging from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The retarding force in this range of rough surface is related to distance by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mi>x</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The speed of the block as it just crosses the rough surface will be
solution:
Acceleration of the block a=fm=-12x2=-6x (Here the block is retarding)

Now, using, a=vdvdx
⇒v dv = a dx
On integrating, we get

 ∫4vvdv=-6∫0.51.5xdx

⇒v2-422=-61.52-0.522

⇒v2=16-12⇒v=2 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
A boy of mass 4 kg is standing on a piece of wood having mass 5 kg. If the coefficient of friction between the wood and the floor is 0.5 , the maximum force that the boy can exert on the rope so that the piece of wood does not move from its place is _______ N. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)

[Take g=10 m s-2
question:
A boy of mass 4 kg is standing on a piece of wood having mass 5 kg. If the coefficient of friction between the wood and the floor is 0.5 , the maximum force that the boy can exert on the rope so that the piece of wood does not move from its place is _______ N. (Round off to the Nearest Integer)

[Take g=10 m s-2
solution:
N+T=90

T=μN=0.5(90-T)

1.5T=45

T=30 N

subject: Physics
q: A block of mass 10 kg is kept on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. A force of 3 N is applied on the block. The coefficient of static friction between the plane and the block is 0.6. What should be the minimum value of force P, such that the block does not move downward? (take g=10 ms-2 )
question: A block of mass 10 kg is kept on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. A force of 3 N is applied on the block. The coefficient of static friction between the plane and the block is 0.6. What should be the minimum value of force P, such that the block does not move downward? (take g=10 ms-2 )
solution:
Block will be at rest

mgsin⁡θ+3N=P+f

10×10×12+3=P+μmgcos⁡θ

( ∵ To get P minimum friction must be minimum)

Pmin=502+3-0.6×10×10×12

=202+3=32N

subject: Physics
q: A monkey of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> climbs on a rope which can withstand the tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>T</mi></mfenced></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. If monkey initially climbs down with an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and then climbs up with an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Choose the correct option <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A monkey of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> climbs on a rope which can withstand the tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>T</mi></mfenced></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. If monkey initially climbs down with an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and then climbs up with an acceleration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Choose the correct option <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
options: Rope will break while climbing upward
Rope will break while going downward
solution:
Let a1 be the acceleration of monkey while moving down.

F.B.D of monkey while moving downward

Using Newton's second law of motion,

mg-T=ma1

∴  500-T=50×4⇒T=300 N

Let a2 be the acceleration of monkey while moving up.

F.B.D of monkey while moving up

Using Newton's second law of motion

T-mg=ma2

⇒  T-500=50×5

⇒T=750 N

Breaking strength of rope =750-300=350 N

∴  Rope will break while monkey is moving upward.
